trajectory_summary: The call is a clean reschedule on the surface — appointment booked, text promised, customer satisfied. Underneath, it is a structural miss: the rep treated a returning lead with a prior no-show and a dense vulnerability disclosure as a pure calendar transaction. No verify items were captured, no bill anchor was attempted, no co-decision-maker was identified or included, and the emotional weight the customer brought to the call was acknowledged with a single 'yeah' before the rep moved to scheduling. The appointment is fragile because the closer will walk in knowing nothing about this customer's qualification status.
outcome_dispute: The appointment was booked, so a 'booked' tag is defensible. However, the booking is structurally fragile: no verify items captured on a prior-no-show lead, daughter identified as a co-decision-maker but not included in the appointment, and the closer has no qualification data to work from. The outcome should be tagged 'booked — fragile' rather than a clean sit.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: and now now my mom is gonna be going to hospice care, to to the hospital for probably about four weeks, and I would like to get everything kinda tidied up and ready for her to come back home for her last days.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The customer disclosed hospice care, personal disability, family loss, and caregiver exhaustion in the first 90 seconds. The rep's response was to move directly to the calendar. On a re-schedule call where the appointment's durability depends on the customer's emotional investment, skipping the acknowledgment is the single highest-risk move the rep made. The manager should use this moment to establish the pattern: vulnerability disclosure is a trust gift that requires a named acknowledgment before any procedural move. This is not about being warm for warmth's sake — it is about appointment durability.

key_moments.quote: and now now my mom is gonna be going to hospice care, to to the hospital for probably about four weeks, and I would like to get everything kinda tidied up and ready for her to come back home for her last days.
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer discloses that her mother is entering hospice for her final weeks of life and that reducing the electric bill is part of preparing the home for her mother's last days. This is a trust gift of the highest order — the customer is telling the rep exactly why this matters emotionally and urgently. The rep's response at 02:12 is 'Yeah. So I can just set up a phone call appointment for you.' No acknowledgment of the hospice disclosure, no bridge to how solar addresses the bill pain she named, no human moment. The reactive read is that the rep simply didn't register the weight of what was said. The strategic read — that the rep was deliberately moving past it to avoid scope creep — does not hold, because empathy acknowledgment is not scope creep; it is the foundation of the appointment's durability.

key_moments.quote: my husband and my daughter right now. So I because I'm disabled myself.
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer identifies both her husband and her daughter as people she must consult before committing to a time — and the daughter is explicitly named as someone she needs to coordinate with for logistics. The schema's adult_child_decision_maker protocol is the more precise match here: the daughter is functioning as a co-decision-maker and logistics coordinator, not just a family member. The rep never asks whether the husband or daughter should be on the appointment. The spouse_decision protocol applies to the husband (title co-owner status unknown); the adult_child_decision_maker protocol applies to the daughter. Neither was addressed. The reactive read is that the rep missed both. The strategic read — that the rep knew this was a phone call and assumed the customer could handle it alone — does not hold, because the customer's own framing ('I have to consult them') signals she does not make decisions independently.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. So I can just set up a phone call appointment for you. It looks like you're already assigned to a specialist. Let me just look at their schedule here.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the moment the rep decides the call is a scheduling transaction. The prior appointment was missed before any qualification occurred — the customer is effectively a new lead from a verify standpoint. The rep skips the entire verify section: no homeowner confirmation, no title/co-owner check, no interest-in-solar depth, no utility company, no bill anchor, no roof age, no prior designs. On a paid lead, the bill is required before the appointment can be set. On any lead type, the closer will walk into this appointment with zero qualification data. This is not a strategic compression of verify beats (as might be justified on a recycled lead with data on file per context.lead_types_and_bill_collection_rules) — there is no evidence any prior data was checked. It is a call_shape failure.

key_moments.quote: And we can get everything tightened, and she can have the last number of days and and be healthy and healthy and not worry well, as as healthy as she can and and not have to worry about all of these things because her her electric bill has just been exorbitant.
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer volunteers the bill pain unprompted and frames it in the most emotionally loaded terms possible — reducing the electric bill is an act of care for her dying mother. This is the bill anchor handing itself to the rep. The rep never picks it up: no 'what does that bill typically run?' follow-up, no flip into alignment-against-the-utility, no acknowledgment that this is the exact problem solar addresses. The bill is mentioned and dropped. Per the schema, capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment. Here, the rep didn't even capture it.
